pi_items.source: Verified in setup_task.sh (line 12), export_result.sh (line 9), and task.json metadata. Path is consistent across all task scripts.
Verified in setup_task.sh (line 13), export_result.sh (line 10), and task.json metadata. Path is consistent across all task scripts.

Verified in verifier.py (line 32) and task.json metadata. The 10,000 vertex threshold is consistent with medical imaging research showing CT cranial base meshes typically have ~5,500 vertices while CBCT models have ~35,000 vertices. The 10,000 threshold represents a reasonable middle ground for 'bona fide skull' quality as stated in verifier.py comment (line 7). Marching Cubes algorithm used by InVesalius produces variable vertex counts depending on source resolution and anatomical complexity.

Verified in verifier.py (line 33) and task.json metadata. The 1.0% tolerance is used in line 98 of verifier.py to check consistency between reported vertex count (from text file) and actual VRML vertex count: 'if percent_diff <= tolerance_percent:'. This is a reasonable tolerance for ensuring data consistency.

privileged_info_summary: The task requires agents to segment a CT cranium DICOM scan using InVesalius 3, generate a bone surface mesh, export it as a VRML 2.0 file to /home/ga/Documents/legacy_skull.wrl, and document the vertex count in /home/ga/Documents/mesh_info.txt. The VRML file must contain valid VRML 2.0 header (#VRML V2.0 utf8) and at least 10,000 vertices to represent a bona fide skull mesh. The reported vertex count in the text file must match the actual VRML vertex count within 1% tolerance. Verification logic parses VRML point coordinates using regex to extract vertex count. Files must be created during task execution (anti-gaming check). Success requires score >= 80 points out of 100, with points awarded for VRML creation (30), mesh complexity meeting 10k vertex minimum (20), info file creation (10), and count consistency within 1% tolerance (40).
